lesson.learn: Comets are icy celestial bodies that originate from the outer reaches of our solar system. As they approach the Sun, they release gas and dust particles, creating a bright tail that can be seen from Earth. This phenomenon is known as a cometary tail, which always points away from the Sun due to the way the comet interacts with solar radiation pressure and the solar wind. The study of comets and their tails has been crucial in understanding the formation and evolution of our solar system.
lesson.simpler: Imagine you're swimming in a pool with a strong current. As you swim, your hair gets blown back by the water, right? That's kind of like what happens to a comet when it approaches the Sun. The heat from the Sun blows away the gas and dust particles surrounding the comet, creating a tail that points away from the Sun.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The key reason comets have tails pointing away from the Sun is due to the combined effect of solar radiation pressure and the solar wind. This interaction causes the gas and dust particles to be blown back, creating the characteristic tail shape.
lesson.deeperDive: Cometary tails are not just a beautiful sight; they hold many secrets about the formation and evolution of our solar system. Recent studies have shown that comets may have delivered organic molecules and water to Earth in the distant past, potentially influencing life's origins. The most famous comet with a highly visible tail is Halley's Comet, which was observed in 1066 AD by Chinese astronomers. Comet Hale-Bopp, seen in 1997, had one of the longest recorded cometary tails, stretching over 5 AU from its nucleus. Comets also have a second type of tail, known as an ion tail, composed of ions and free electrons that interact with the solar wind.
lesson.funFact: Did you know that the shortest recorded comet was Comet ISON, which disintegrated in just 2 days after approaching the Sun in 2013? The longest recorded cometary tail belongs to Comet Hale-Bopp, stretching over 300 million kilometers (186 million miles) from its nucleus!

people: * Dr. Halley (English astronomer who predicted the return of the comet)
* Dr. Shoemaker-Levy 9 (Comet that broke apart and collided with Jupiter in 1994)
* Dr. Don Yeomans (Director of NASA's Near-Earth Object Program Office)
* Dr. Erisa Schmitt (Planetary scientist studying comets at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory)
* Dr. Alan Hale (Astronomer who discovered Comet Hale-Bopp in 1995)
places: * The United Kingdom Observatory, where Halley first observed his comet
* Lowell Observatory, where the discovery of Pluto was announced in 1930
* Green Bank Telescope, used to study cometary tails and origins
* NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, leading research on comets and their interactions with solar radiation
years: * 1066 AD - Chinese astronomers observe Halley's Comet
* 1997 - Comet Hale-Bopp is seen with the longest recorded cometary tail
* 2013 - Comet ISON disintegrates just 2 days after approaching the Sun
* 1910 - Halley's Comet last appeared in the sky, prompting massive astronomical observations
concepts: * Solar radiation pressure: force exerted by sunlight on particles and objects
* Solar wind: stream of charged particles emitted by the Sun
* Cometary nucleus: solid core of a comet containing ice and dust
* Gas and dust particles: released when a comet approaches the Sun, creating its tail
* Ion tail: second type of cometary tail composed of ions and free electrons
